name: Maize (Grain)
notes: Dent/flint maize for dry grain storage and flour (not sweet corn). Warm-season annual. Very high-yielding starch staple. Can be dried and stored whole year.

name: Mulberry (Black)
notes: Morus nigra — richest flavour, highest anthocyanins. Needs warm summers. Shorter stature than M. alba. Fruits do not transport well — best picked fresh.

name: Mulberry (White)
notes: Morus alba — widest climate range of all mulberries. Very high yielding, mild sweet flavour. Traditional silkworm host. Tolerates drought, alkaline soils, and moderate frost. Good for arid and continental climates.

name: Mulberry (Red)
notes: Morus rubra — native to eastern North America. More cold-hardy than M. nigra. Fruits are tart-sweet, excellent fresh or dried. Best suited to temperate and continental climates. Prefers moist, well-drained soils.

name: Mulberry (Long / Shahtoot)
notes: Morus macroura (syn. M. alba var. multicaulis) — Himalayan/South Asian long mulberry (Shahtoot). Fruits 5–10 cm long, very sweet. Suited to warm subtropics and warm temperate. High yielding; popular in South Asia and Middle East.

name: Olive (Oil)
notes: Oil varieties (Frantoio, Picual, Arbequina). Yield expressed as extracted oil — approx 15–20% of fruit weight. labour_hand includes hand-harvest and press.

name: Olive (Table)
notes: Table varieties (Kalamata, Ligurian, Manzanilla). Harvested green or ripe; yield as whole pickled fruit. Higher hand-labour for careful picking and brining.

name: Tomato
notes: yield_high reflects intensive open-field production (staked/trellised). Greenhouse hydroponics can reach 300-500 t/ha but is not modelled here.

name: Walnut
food_group: Lean Meats, Eggs, Nuts, Seeds & Legumes

name: Wattle Seed (Native)
food_group: Lean Meats, Eggs, Nuts, Seeds & Legumes

name: White Oak
notes: Acorns are low-tannin, suitable for flour after minimal leaching. Primary starch food from forest gardens.

name: Burr Oak
notes: Quercus macrocarpa — largest native North American acorn. Extremely cold hardy. Very low tannin; can be eaten with minimal processing. Excellent starchy staple for cold temperate and continental climates.

name: Holm Oak
notes: Quercus ilex — evergreen Mediterranean oak. Sweet acorns (sweet bellota variety) with low tannin, eaten raw or roasted. Also used for Ibérico pig pannage. Highly drought tolerant. Excellent for arid-mediterranean food forests.

name: Winged Bean
food_group: Lean Meats, Eggs, Nuts, Seeds & Legumes

name: Plantain
notes: Cooking banana (Musa × paradisiaca). Primarily eaten cooked — boiled, baked, or fried as a starchy staple. Can replace grains and root vegetables. Very high-yielding perennial for humid tropics and subtropics. Harvest year-round in good conditions.

name: Ensete
notes: False banana (Ensete ventricosum). Ethiopian staple — the pseudostem and corm are scraped, fermented 1–6 months to produce kocho (flatbread/porridge) and bulla (starch extract). Extraordinarily productive per hectare. Frost-sensitive; thrives in subtropical highlands 1500–3000m. Critical food security crop for humid upland tropics.

name: Yacon
notes: Andean ground apple (Smallanthus sonchifolius). Crisp, sweet, jicama-like tubers eaten raw or cooked. Very high in fructooligosaccharides (FOS) — a prebiotic fibre; low glycaemic despite apparent carb content. Excellent for temperate and cool subtropical gardens. Plant rhizomes in spring, harvest autumn after frost kills tops.

name: Turmeric
notes: Plant rhizomes in spring; harvest winter when leaves die back. Can be used fresh or dried and ground. Also an important medicinal crop (curcumin). Grows well in subtropical Australia.

name: Galangal
notes: Greater galangal (Alpinia galanga). Aromatic rhizome essential in Southeast Asian cooking. More heat- and moisture-demanding than ginger. Thrives in humid subtropical Australia. Harvest rhizomes after 8–12 months; replant offsets.

name: Lemongrass
notes: Cymbopogon citratus. Edible lower stalk used as aromatic vegetable in Asian cooking. Also valuable for essential oil, lemon-scented tea, and pest repellent. Harvest stalks year-round in warm climates; divide clumps annually to maintain vigour. Very drought-tolerant once established.

name: Malabar Spinach
notes: Basella alba. The key heat-tolerant spinach substitute for tropical and subtropical gardens where regular spinach bolts in summer. Climbing vine; train on trellis. Leaves are mucilaginous when cooked — excellent in soups and stir-fries. Extraordinarily high vitamin C and iron.

name: Warrigal Greens
notes: Tetragonia tetragonioides. Native Australian and NZ spinach substitute. Sprawling ground cover; harvest young tips and leaves. Must be blanched to reduce oxalates. Highly drought-tolerant and salt-tolerant — thrives in coastal gardens. Used as a vegetable by First Nations Australians and early European settlers.

name: Dragon Fruit
notes: Selenicereus undatus (white flesh). Climbing cactus; requires a sturdy post or trellis and cross-pollination for good fruit set. Very drought tolerant once established. Multiple flushes per year in warm climates; harvest 30–50 days after flowering. Suits arid and semi-arid Australia well.

name: Oca
notes: Oxalis tuberosa. Andean tuber forming small, colourful rhizomes. Short-day obligate — tubers only form as days shorten in autumn; harvest after first frost kills tops. Can be eaten raw (slightly sour from oxalic acid) or cooked. Sun-drying reduces acidity and sweetens the tuber. Excellent cold-climate alternative to potato.

name: Shallot
notes: Allium cepa var. aggregatum. Milder, more complex flavour than onion; each bulb divides into 4–8 cloves for replanting. Cool-season crop; bolt-resistant when planted in autumn. Widely grown in Australian home gardens. Store bulbs in a dry, airy location after curing.

name: Acerola Cherry
notes: Malpighia emarginata. The richest natural source of vitamin C on Earth — up to 65× more than orange per gram. Tart cherry-like fruit; usually processed into juice, powder, or jam rather than eaten fresh due to acidity. Multiple flushes per year. Widely grown in Queensland and northern NSW.

name: Jaboticaba
notes: Plinia cauliflora. Brazilian grape tree — clusters of dark purple berries erupt directly from the trunk and branches (cauliflory). Multiple crops per year once mature. Fruit must be processed within 3–4 days; excellent for jam, wine, and juice. Slow-growing but long-lived. Very popular in subtropical Australian food gardens.

name: Ice Cream Bean
notes: Inga edulis. Fast-growing nitrogen-fixing legume tree. The sweet white cottony pulp surrounding the seeds has a texture and flavour reminiscent of vanilla ice cream. Pods 30–90 cm long. Fruits mainly in the dry season (April–September in subtropical Aus). Excellent multi-purpose agroforestry tree — provides shade, mulch, and food. Seeds are also edible boiled.

name: Kiwano
notes: Cucumis metuliferus. Horned melon from sub-Saharan Africa; one of the few cucurbits that stores for months at room temperature without refrigeration. Distinctive spiky orange skin; lime-green jelly-like interior with cucumber-banana flavour. Drought tolerant. Excellent for hot, dry Australian climates. Eat by cutting in half and scooping out the pulp.
